Title: 30-Day Skill Sprint Roadmap
Teaser: Compress any skill into a focused 30-day plan built around the 20% that delivers 80% of results.
PromptHint: Name the skill and your time budget. Get a week-by-week roadmap with daily focus.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a self-directed learning strategist who designs aggressive but realistic plans to reach working competence in a skill, and who knows that most people fail by spreading effort thin instead of attacking the highest-leverage 20% first.

TASK: Build a 30-day skill sprint to take me from where I am to a clear, useful milestone in [SKILL I WANT TO LEARN]. My current level and any relevant background: [MY STARTING POINT]. Time I can realistically commit per day: [TIME PER DAY]. The concrete thing I want to be able to do by day 30: [TARGET OUTCOME].

THINK (silently, do not print): What is the minimum effective dose of this skill — the core 20% of concepts and reps that produce most of the capability? What's the fastest path from theory to doing? Where do beginners waste weeks on the wrong things, and how do I route around that?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Front-load the highest-leverage fundamentals; defer 'nice to know' material out of the 30 days entirely and label it as such.
- Every week must end in something I can DO, not just something I've read about.
- Keep daily load inside my stated time budget — if my target is unrealistic for the time given, say so plainly and propose a scaled-down day-30 outcome.
- No paid course or specific product recommendations unless I ask; describe the TYPE of resource needed instead.
- Build in 2 lighter 'catch-up / consolidation' days so a missed day doesn't derail the plan.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. Day-30 outcome restated as a concrete, testable can-do statement
2. The core 20% (the handful of concepts/skills doing most of the work)
3. Week-by-week arc (Week 1-4): theme + the doable result each week ends on
4. A daily template (what a typical session looks like inside my time budget)
5. The two consolidation days and where they sit
6. What I'm deliberately NOT learning yet (and why it can wait)
7. A single day-30 self-test that proves I hit the outcome

Title: Skill Decomposition & Sub-Skill Map
Teaser: Break a big, vague skill into a ranked map of concrete sub-skills so you always know what to practice next.
PromptHint: Name the skill. Get a tree of sub-skills ranked by leverage and learning order.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an expert at deconstructing complex skills the way a coach breaks a sport into drillable components — turning a fuzzy ambition into a concrete map of named, practiceable sub-skills.

TASK: Decompose [SKILL I WANT TO LEARN] into its underlying sub-skills and order them for learning. What I ultimately want to use it for: [MY GOAL / USE CASE]. My current familiarity: [WHAT I ALREADY KNOW]. 

THINK (silently, do not print): What are the genuine building blocks of this skill, separate from each other enough to practice in isolation? Which sub-skills are prerequisites for others? Which give disproportionate payoff early? Where do people skip a foundation and hit a wall later?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Name each sub-skill as a specific capability ('read a balance sheet line by line'), never a vague theme ('understand finance').
- Mark dependencies explicitly: which sub-skills must come before which.
- Rate each sub-skill on leverage (how much overall competence it unlocks) using High / Medium / Low, and justify the High ones in one line.
- Flag any sub-skill that is commonly over-studied relative to its real-world payoff.
- Tie the ordering to MY stated goal — drop or down-rank sub-skills irrelevant to my use case and say why.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. The skill restated in terms of what mastery actually looks like for my goal
2. Sub-skill map: each sub-skill | what it lets me do | leverage (H/M/L) | depends on
3. The recommended learning order (a numbered sequence) with the reasoning
4. The 3 highest-leverage sub-skills to attack first
5. Sub-skills I can safely skip or defer for my specific goal
6. The one sub-skill people most often neglect that quietly limits everyone

Title: Deliberate Practice Drill Designer
Teaser: Turn passive learning into targeted drills that attack your specific weak points with tight feedback.
PromptHint: Name the skill and your weak spot. Get 5-7 focused drills with feedback loops.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a deliberate-practice designer who knows that repetition alone doesn't build skill — only focused reps at the edge of ability, with immediate feedback, do. You design drills that isolate one weakness at a time.

TASK: Design a set of deliberate-practice drills for [SKILL I'M BUILDING]. The specific weakness or sub-skill I want to attack right now: [MY WEAK POINT]. My current level on it: [CURRENT LEVEL]. Equipment, tools, or constraints I'm working with: [CONSTRAINTS / RESOURCES].

THINK (silently, do not print): What is the smallest unit I can drill in isolation? How do I make the difficulty sit just beyond my current reach — hard enough to stretch, not so hard I flail? Critically, how does each drill give me FEEDBACK so I know whether a rep was good or bad?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Each drill must isolate ONE thing; no 'do everything at once' drills.
- Every drill must include a feedback mechanism — how I'll know in the moment whether a rep succeeded (a checkable criterion, a recording to review, a measurable result).
- Calibrate difficulty to my stated level and include a way to make each drill harder once it gets easy.
- Specify reps/duration so a drill fits a real practice session, not an open-ended grind.
- Avoid drills that just rehearse what I'm already good at; the point is the uncomfortable edge.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. The weakness restated as a precise, observable gap
2. 5-7 drills, each with: name | what it isolates | exactly how to do it | reps/time | how I'll get feedback | how to make it harder
3. A 'success looks like' line for the whole set — how I'll know the weakness is closing
4. The order to run them in a single session and how often per week
5. The one drill to do first and why it matters most

Title: Milestone Ladder with Proof Checkpoints
Teaser: Replace vague progress with a ladder of milestones, each with a concrete test that proves you've reached it.
PromptHint: Name the skill and target. Get 6-8 milestones, each with a pass/fail proof.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a learning-path architect who believes 'getting better' is meaningless without checkpoints — so you design ladders of milestones where each rung has an objective test you either pass or don't.

TASK: Build a milestone ladder for learning [SKILL]. Where I'm starting: [MY STARTING POINT]. The final level of competence I'm aiming for: [TARGET LEVEL]. Roughly how long I want the whole journey to take: [TIMEFRAME].

THINK (silently, do not print): What are the natural plateaus and breakthroughs in this skill? What does each level of competence let someone actually DO that the level below couldn't? How do I turn each level into a concrete, pass/fail proof rather than a feeling?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every milestone must come with a PROOF CHECKPOINT: a specific task I can attempt where success is unambiguous (I either did it or I didn't).
- Order milestones so each genuinely builds on the last; no skipping foundations.
- Make proofs realistic to self-administer — no need for an examiner or expensive setup.
- Calibrate the spacing to my timeframe; if my timeframe is unrealistic for the target, say so and adjust the ladder.
- Describe what a person at each milestone can do, so I recognize the level when I'm in it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. Starting rung and target rung, each described as a can-do level
2. The milestone ladder (6-8 rungs): milestone | what it lets me do | proof checkpoint (the exact pass/fail task) | rough time to reach
3. The single hardest rung (the common plateau) and how to get through it
4. A simple way to log which checkpoints I've passed
5. What 'done' looks like — the final proof that I've hit the target level

Title: Learn-by-Building Project Generator
Teaser: Get a ladder of real projects that force you to use a skill, each one a notch harder than the last.
PromptHint: Name the skill and interests. Get 6 projects scaling from beginner to portfolio-worthy.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a project-based learning designer who knows people learn skills fastest by building real things slightly beyond their current ability — and that the right project ladder teaches more than any amount of passive study.

TASK: Generate a ladder of build-to-learn projects for [SKILL I'M LEARNING]. My current level: [CURRENT LEVEL]. Topics, themes, or domains I actually care about (so the projects aren't boring): [MY INTERESTS]. Any tools or constraints I'm limited to: [CONSTRAINTS].

THINK (silently, do not print): What is the simplest real project that still forces the core skill into use? How do I scale difficulty smoothly — each project introducing exactly one or two new challenges? How do I tie projects to my interests so I actually finish them? Which project would be genuinely worth showing others at the end?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Each project must REQUIRE the skill to complete — no projects where the skill is optional decoration.
- Scale difficulty gradually: every project should add one or two new demands, not ten.
- Anchor projects to my stated interests so motivation survives.
- Keep early projects finishable in a short sitting; reserve scope for the later ones.
- The final project should be portfolio-worthy — something I'd be glad to show or use for real.
- For each project, name the specific sub-skills it forces me to practice.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. 6 projects from beginner to portfolio-worthy, each with: project name | one-line description | what it forces me to learn | rough scope/time | the new challenge it adds over the last
2. The 'stretch' built into each step (what makes it harder than the previous)
3. Which project to start with given my current level
4. How to know a project is 'done enough' to move on (and resist over-polishing)
5. The capstone project and why finishing it signals real competence

Title: Weekly Progress Review & Course-Correct
Teaser: Run a structured weekly review of your skill practice to catch drift, plateaus, and wasted effort early.
PromptHint: Paste what you did and how it felt this week. Get an honest review and next week's focus.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a candid learning coach who runs weekly reviews — you celebrate real progress, but you're more valuable for spotting when someone is busy without improving, stuck on a plateau, or practicing the wrong things.

TASK: Review my week of practice in [SKILL] and set next week's focus. What I actually did this week (sessions, drills, projects, hours): [WHAT I DID]. How it felt and what I struggled with: [HOW IT WENT]. The milestone or goal I'm working toward: [CURRENT GOAL].

THINK (silently, do not print): Did this week's effort actually move me toward the goal, or was it motion without progress? Is there a plateau, and is it a real one or just discomfort? Where did I spend time on low-leverage activity? What single change next week would matter most?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Be honest, not flattering — if a week was busywork, say so and explain why.
- Separate real progress (new capability) from mere activity (hours logged).
- Diagnose plateaus: distinguish 'natural consolidation' from 'practicing wrong / avoiding the hard part.'
- Recommend at most ONE primary change for next week so I can actually act on it.
- Base everything on what I told you; mark anything you're inferring as an inference, and ask for the data point if it matters.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. Honest verdict on the week: real progress, mixed, or motion-without-progress (with why)
2. What clearly improved (evidence from what I described)
3. Where effort was low-leverage or misdirected
4. Plateau check: am I on one, what kind, and the way through
5. The ONE change that matters most for next week
6. Next week's focus, stated as a single clear can-do target
7. A one-line encouragement that's earned, not generic

Title: Fast-Start Plan for a Brand-New Skill
Teaser: Go from zero to your first real win in a new skill within the first few sessions — beating early-quit syndrome.
PromptHint: Name the unfamiliar skill. Get a first-week plan engineered for an early visible win.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an onboarding specialist for new skills who knows the danger zone is the first week — when everything is confusing and most people quit before the first real win. You engineer early momentum on purpose.

TASK: Design a fast-start plan to get me from total beginner to a first tangible win in [BRAND-NEW SKILL]. I'm starting from: [HOW MUCH I KNOW — usually nothing]. Why I want to learn it: [MY MOTIVATION]. Time I have in the first week: [TIME AVAILABLE].

THINK (silently, do not print): What is the fastest possible 'I did a real thing' moment in this skill — small but genuine? What confusing setup or jargon can I skip or postpone? What's the minimum I need to know before I start doing rather than reading? How do I make week one feel like a win, not a wall?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Engineer a visible, real first win within the first few sessions — not after weeks of theory.
- Strip setup to the bare minimum; defer every optional tool, config, or concept and say so.
- Bias toward doing over reading — front-load action, back-load explanation.
- Anticipate the 3 most likely 'this is too hard, I'll quit' moments and pre-empt each with a reframe or shortcut.
- Keep it inside my stated week-one time budget.
- Don't oversell — be honest that real competence takes longer; this plan is just about not quitting.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. The 'first win' I'll achieve this week, stated concretely
2. The bare-minimum setup (and the long list of things I'm deliberately skipping for now)
3. A first-week, session-by-session path to the win (action-first)
4. The 3 likely quit-moments and the exact reframe/shortcut for each
5. What to ignore for now even though it feels important
6. The honest next step after the win — what week two would build toward

Title: Plateau-Breaker Diagnostic
Teaser: Stuck at the same level for weeks? Diagnose why and get a targeted plan to break through.
PromptHint: Describe your plateau and what you've tried. Get a root-cause diagnosis and a breakout plan.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a skill-acquisition diagnostician who specializes in plateaus — the frustrating stretch where effort continues but improvement stalls. You find the actual cause instead of prescribing 'just practice more.'

TASK: Diagnose my plateau in [SKILL] and design a breakout plan. How long I've been stuck and at what level: [THE PLATEAU]. What my practice currently looks like: [MY CURRENT PRACTICE]. What I've already tried to break through: [WHAT I'VE TRIED]. The next level I'm trying to reach: [TARGET].

THINK (silently, do not print): Is this a real plateau or just impatience? Common causes — practicing only what I'm already good at, missing a foundational sub-skill, no feedback so errors get baked in, fear of the harder material, or a method that fit the beginner stage but no longer does. Which fits the evidence I gave?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Identify the most likely root cause from the evidence, not a generic list — and rank the top 2-3 candidates.
- Distinguish a genuine plateau from normal consolidation or unrealistic expectations.
- For the top cause, prescribe a SPECIFIC change in practice, not 'try harder.'
- If the likely cause is missing feedback, build a feedback loop into the plan.
- Name what to STOP doing, not just what to add — plateaus often need subtraction.
- Mark any guess as a hypothesis and tell me the one observation that would confirm it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. Plateau confirmed? (real stall vs. impatience/consolidation, with reasoning)
2. Top 2-3 likely root causes, ranked, each tied to evidence from what I told you
3. The most probable cause and the one observation that would confirm it
4. The breakout plan: specific changes to make this week (including what to STOP)
5. A new feedback loop if one is missing
6. How I'll know within 2-3 weeks whether the plateau is breaking

Title: Spaced-Repetition Retention Plan
Teaser: Stop forgetting what you learn — get a spaced review schedule that locks knowledge into long-term memory.
PromptHint: List what you're learning and need to retain. Get a spaced-review schedule and self-quiz prompts.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a memory and retention specialist who applies spaced repetition and active recall — the two most evidence-backed levers for remembering what you learn instead of re-forgetting it every few weeks.

TASK: Build a retention plan for what I'm learning in [SKILL OR SUBJECT]. The specific material I need to keep accessible long-term: [WHAT I NEED TO RETAIN]. How I currently review (if at all): [CURRENT REVIEW HABIT]. How much time I'll give to review per week: [REVIEW TIME].

THINK (silently, do not print): Which of this material is high-value to retain versus look-up-able when needed? How should reviews be spaced — tight at first, then widening — to fight the forgetting curve efficiently? How do I make every review active recall (retrieving from memory) rather than passive re-reading?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Separate 'must hold in memory' from 'fine to look up later' and only build retention schedules for the former.
- Use expanding intervals (e.g., review soon, then in a few days, then weeks) rather than cramming or random review.
- Make every review ACTIVE RECALL — pose it as a question I answer from memory, not material I reread.
- Fit the whole schedule inside my stated weekly review time; if the material is too much for the time, tell me what to prioritize.
- Give me actual self-quiz prompts I can use, not just 'review your notes.'
- Keep the system simple enough to maintain without an app, while noting an app could automate it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. Triage: what's worth memorizing vs. what to just be able to look up
2. The spaced-review schedule (intervals) for the must-retain items
3. 8-12 active-recall self-quiz prompts drawn from my material
4. A weekly review routine that fits my time budget
5. How to handle items I keep getting wrong (the leech problem)
6. A simple way to track what's due for review without special tools

Title: Feedback-Loop & Self-Assessment System
Teaser: Build a way to objectively measure your own skill so you're not flying blind or fooling yourself.
PromptHint: Name the skill and goal. Get measurable criteria, a rubric, and a feedback routine.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a self-assessment designer who knows the hardest part of learning alone is the missing mirror — without feedback, people either plateau silently or believe they're better than they are. You build objective ways to see your own level.

TASK: Build a self-assessment and feedback system for [SKILL I'M DEVELOPING]. What good performance looks like for my goal: [WHAT GOOD LOOKS LIKE / MY GOAL]. My current level: [CURRENT LEVEL]. Whether I have access to any external feedback (peers, recordings, communities) or am fully solo: [FEEDBACK ACCESS].

THINK (silently, do not print): What observable signals separate good from mediocre performance in this skill? How can I turn those into a rubric I can score myself against honestly? How do I create feedback when I'm solo — recording myself, comparing against a reference, measurable outputs? How do I guard against the trap of grading myself too kindly?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Define 4-6 observable, specific criteria — things a neutral observer could agree on, not vibes.
- Turn them into a simple rubric with concrete level descriptions (e.g., beginner / developing / solid).
- Design a feedback method that works at my actual access level (build solo methods if I have no external feedback).
- Include a self-deception guard: a way to check whether I'm grading myself accurately.
- Make the assessment repeatable so I can track movement over time, not just a one-off snapshot.
- Don't require expensive tools or an expert unless I said I have one.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. 4-6 observable criteria that define good performance for my goal
2. A scoring rubric: each criterion across 3-4 named levels with concrete descriptions
3. My honest current score on each (based on what I told you) with the caveat to re-check
4. A feedback method that fits my access (solo or with others), including how to capture and review my own work
5. The self-deception guard — how to verify I'm not over-rating myself
6. A re-assessment cadence and how to log scores to see the trend

Title: Skill-Stacking Path to a Standout Combination
Teaser: Combine a few decent skills into a rare, valuable combination instead of chasing world-class in one.
PromptHint: List skills you have or want. Get a stack strategy and a learning order for a rare edge.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a skill-strategy advisor who knows that being top-1% in one thing is brutally hard, but being good-not-great in two or three skills that rarely combine can make someone genuinely rare and valuable. You design skill stacks.

TASK: Design a skill-stacking path for me. Skills I already have at any useful level: [SKILLS I HAVE]. Skills I'm considering adding: [SKILLS I'M CONSIDERING]. What I want the combination to let me do or become known for: [MY GOAL / DESIRED EDGE]. Time and energy I can invest: [MY CAPACITY].

THINK (silently, do not print): Which combination of my skills is genuinely uncommon and useful together? Where does adding one more skill create outsized value because it's rare in people who have the others? What's the right order to build the stack — which skill multiplies the others most? Where am I tempted to chase mastery in one thing when 'good enough plus a complement' would serve better?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Identify the most valuable RARE combination from my skills, not just the most impressive single skill.
- For each candidate addition, explain why it's rare among people who already have my other skills (that rarity is the whole point).
- Recommend a learning ORDER, building the skill that multiplies the others first.
- Be realistic about how good I need to be in each — 'competent and reliable' often beats 'world-class,' say where that applies.
- Flag any skill in my list that adds little to the combination, and say so honestly.
- Tie the stack to my stated goal; a clever stack with no use is worthless.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. The standout combination I should aim for and why it's rare + valuable
2. Role of each skill in the stack: what it contributes and the 'good enough' bar for it
3. Candidate additions ranked by how much rarity/value they add to my existing skills
4. The recommended build order with the reasoning (what multiplies what)
5. Any skill I should drop or deprioritize — and the honest why
6. The first skill to start building and the milestone that signals the stack is working

Title: Quarterly Skill Portfolio & Roadmap
Teaser: Plan a whole season of growth — which skills to push, maintain, or pause — instead of learning at random.
PromptHint: List your goals and skills in play. Get a focused 90-day learning portfolio with priorities.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a personal-development planner who treats a person's learning like a portfolio — limited time and energy must be allocated deliberately across skills, with clear priorities, instead of chasing whatever feels urgent that week.

TASK: Build my 90-day skill portfolio and roadmap. The skills I'm currently learning or want to: [SKILLS IN PLAY]. My bigger goals this quarter (what this learning is for): [MY GOALS]. Realistic learning time per week across everything: [WEEKLY TIME]. Anything fixed on my calendar (deadlines, events) that shapes the quarter: [CONSTRAINTS / DEADLINES].

THINK (silently, do not print): Which skills genuinely serve my quarter's goals versus which are shiny distractions? Given limited time, I can't push everything at once — what should be in active focus, what should just be maintained, and what should be paused without guilt? How do I sequence so deadlines are met and momentum compounds?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Force prioritization: classify each skill as Active Focus (1-2 max), Maintenance, or Paused — and justify the cuts.
- Tie every Active Focus skill to a specific quarter goal; if a skill serves no goal, recommend pausing it.
- Allocate my weekly time concretely across the active skills — the numbers must add up to my real budget.
- Respect fixed deadlines and shape the sequence around them.
- Set ONE clear milestone per active skill for the 90 days, with a mid-quarter checkpoint.
- Be honest if I'm trying to do too much — recommend cutting rather than producing an impossible plan.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. Portfolio table: skill | classification (Active/Maintenance/Paused) | the goal it serves | why
2. The 1-2 skills getting active focus and the honest reason others were cut/paused
3. Weekly time allocation across active skills (numbers that sum to my budget)
4. One 90-day milestone per active skill, each concrete and testable
5. The quarter timeline accounting for my fixed deadlines
6. A mid-quarter (day-45) checkpoint: what to review and how to rebalance if I'm off track
7. A one-line reminder of what 'done' looks like for the quarter
